div1.entryFree:
Bruise, vb., 1) to hurt by a contusion: I throw thy name against the --ing stones, Gentl. I, 2, 111. I --d my shin, Wiv. I, 1, 294. falling from a hill, he was so --d, H4A V, 5, 21. Applied to defensive arms, == to dint: --d arms, Lucr. 110. R3 I, 1, 6. his --d helmet, H5 V Chor. H5 V Chor. --d pieces, Ant. IV, 14, 42. The same sense in the following passages: we thought not good to b. an injury till it were fully ripe, H5 III, 6, 129 (to open an ulcer by squeezing it). Palamedes sore hurt and --d, Troil. V, 5, 14. they yet glance by and scarcely b. Lr. V, 3, 148 (though it seems here to be == to hurt, to wound in general).
2) to crush, to grind, to destroy: let us be keen and rather cut a little than fall and b. to death, Meas. II, 1, 6. a wretched soul, --d with adversity, Err. II, 1, 34. b. me with scorn, LLL V, 2, 397. b. her flowerets with the armed hoofs of hostile paces, H4A I, 1, 8. leads ancient lords to bloody battles and to --ing arms, III, 2, 105. --d underneath the yoke of tyranny, R3 V, 2, 2. put in their hands thy --ing irons of wrath, V, 3, 110. his contempt shall not be --ing to you, when he hath power to crush, Cor. II, 3, 210. the law shall b. him, Tim. III, 5, 4. that the --d heart was pierced through the ear, Oth. I, 3, 219.
